title: Time-boundary scattering and topological resonant transmissions
abstract: Time boundaries (TBs), temporal analogues of spatial interfaces, offer a powerful handle to engineer quantum systems. However, unlike the well-developed stationary scattering theory at spatial interfaces, a unified framework for quantum scattering at TBs has been missing. Here we develop a Bloch-wave scattering theory for TBs by introducing a temporal scattering matrix $S$ between incoming and outgoing Bloch channels. We uncover topological resonant transmissions (RTs) -- poles of $S$ that yield perfect interband transmission and dynamical freezing of the quantum state. We establish a bulk-time-boundary correspondence for all integer Altland-Zirnbauer classes: the number of RTs equals the jump of the bulk topological invariant across the TB. In one dimension this gives a time-domain Levinson's theorem. A topological analysis further reveals a striking dimensional dependence. In even dimensions RTs are robust to temporal modulations and disorder, whereas in odd dimensions they can be destroyed by dynamical symmetry breaking. Our work places temporal and spatial scattering on the same footing and opens new avenues for engineering and probing quantum dynamics.
summary: This paper establishes a unified scattering theory for quantum systems undergoing temporal transitions. It identifies 'topological resonant transmissions' that allow perfect state transfer between bands and proves that the number of these resonances is protected by the change in the system's bulk topology.
main_problem: The lack of a unified scattering framework for quantum systems at time-boundaries (TBs), which are temporal analogues of spatial interfaces.
main_result: The development of a Bloch-wave scattering theory for TBs, establishing a bulk-time-boundary correspondence where the number of topological resonant transmissions (RTs) equals the jump in the bulk topological invariant.
method: The authors introduce a temporal scattering matrix (S-matrix) and transfer matrix (M-matrix) approach, treating the time-varying region as a scattering interface between two static phases.
model_or_system: A d-dimensional lattice system with a piecewise time-dependent Hamiltonian H(t) that transitions between two static bulk phases (H_L and H_R) over a duration tau_B.
key_observables: S-matrix elements (reflection/transmission amplitudes), inter-channel transmission probability, birefringence, and dynamical freezing of the quantum state.
important_parameters: TB duration (tau_B), topological invariants (Chern number, winding number), and Altland-Zirnbauer symmetry classes.
main_assumptions: The framework focuses on integer Altland-Zirnbauer classes and assumes a gap separating valence and conduction bands.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares spatial and temporal scattering; Figure 4 shows the effect of disorder on transmission; Figure 5 illustrates the impact of symmetry-breaking temporal modulation.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of time-boundaries, develops the Bloch-wave scattering theory, establishes the bulk-time-boundary correspondence, analyzes dimensional dependence and robustness, and extends the analysis to higher-order poles and 3D systems.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful new tool for engineering and probing quantum dynamics, specifically offering a way to identify topological phases in cold atom systems by observing scattering patterns at high-symmetry points.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Spatial versus temporal scattering. (a) In real space, a plane wave of fixed energy impinges on a potential V (x), leading to re- flected and transmitted waves and possibly bound states. The pro- cess is governed by stationary scattering theory. (b) In time, the Hamiltonian changes from HL to HR across a time boundary (TB). Bloch waves of definite crystal momentum act as scattering chan- nels, while energy is not conserved. A TB of duration τB generates a unitary evolution UB and the scattering obeys the Schr¨odinger equa- tion. Arrows pointing towards (away from) the TB indicate incoming (outgoing) waves, and colors distinguish states before and after the boundary.

FIG. 2. Resonant transmission (RT) across a TB for the 2D model in Eq. (7). (a) Schematic of the TB with time-varying mass terms mx(t) and mz(t). Band structures on either side are shown in blue and purple. (b) Trajectory of the poles of the S-matrix in the Bril- louin zone (BZ) as the TB duration τB increases (indicated by the arrow). Contour plots show the inter-channel transmission T−+ for two different τB values. The brightest points mark the RT. (c) Time evolution on the Bloch sphere for the RT mode (red) and an off- resonant mode k = (0.5, 0) (black). The system starts in the ground state of HL (black stars). The evolution inside and after the TB is shown by the solid and dashed...

FIG. 3. Topological view of RT in the elementary Clifford represen- tation. (a) For integer AZ classes in dD, the topological invariant is the mapping degree from the Brillouin zone (T d) to the d sphere (Sd) spanned by the normalized Hamiltonian vector ⃗h. A RT arises at a quenched TB when ⃗hL and ⃗hR are antiparallel. (b) Stability of RTs in even dimensions. Temporal modulations inside the TB can stretch the mapping encoded by the effective Hamiltonian Heff(k, t), but cannot alter the global topology without gap closings.

FIG. 5. RT in the extended SSH model (15) and dynamical symmetry breaking. (a) Phase diagram with topologically distinct phases (col- ored regions) labeled by the winding number w1. Arrows indicate the three quenched TBs. (b) Inter-channel transmission probability T−+ versus momentum k for different TB settings. The blue, or- ange, and gray lines correspond to the quenched protocols 1⃝2⃝and 3⃝in (a). The red and dashed red lines are for temporal modulation HT B(t) = σz and HT B(t) = (1 −t/τB)HL + t/τBHR + σz, respectively.
